title: TTP and HUS: Thrombotic Microangiopathies
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Hematology Fellows, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize the clinical features of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA)
Distinguish TTP from HUS and other causes of MAHA with thrombocytopenia
Apply the PLASMIC score for TTP risk stratification
Understand ADAMTS13 deficiency in TTP pathophysiology
Initiate plasma exchange and appropriate therapy for TTP
Differentiate typical (STEC) HUS from atypical (complement-mediated) HUS

guidelineReferences.name: ISTH TTP Diagnosis Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Zheng XL, et al. ISTH guidelines for the diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. J Thromb Haemost. 2020;18:2486-2495
keyPoints: PLASMIC or French score for pretest probability
Start plasma exchange before ADAMTS13 results if high clinical suspicion
Add caplacizumab, rituximab, steroids for confirmed iTTP

guidelineReferences.name: StatPearls: TTP
guidelineReferences.citation: Joly BS, et al. Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura. StatPearls 2023

guidelineReferences.name: Korean aHUS Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Cheong HI, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome. J Korean Med Sci. 2016;31:1516-1528

vignette.text:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the ED with confusion, fatigue, and bruising for 2 days. She reports headaches and noted dark urine yesterday. She denies diarrhea, fever, or recent illness. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications.

On arrival, she is disoriented to time and place. Family reports she was completely normal 3 days ago.

Past Medical History: None

Medications: None

Allergies: None

Social History: Works as an accountant, no tobacco, occasional alcohol, no recent travel

physicalExam.neuro: Disoriented to time and place, follows simple commands, no focal deficits
physicalExam.skin: Scattered petechiae on trunk and extremities, ecchymoses on arms, mild jaundice

labs.Peripheral Smear: Numerous schistocytes (3-4 per HPF), decreased platelets, polychromasia

questions.stem: This patient has severe thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, and neurologic changes.
questions.question: What defines a thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) and what is the differential diagnosis?
questions.hint: Consider the triad of findings and what causes them.

options.text: TMA = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) + thrombocytopenia + organ injury; Differential: TTP (ADAMTS13 deficiency), HUS (typical/STEC vs atypical/complement), DIC, HELLP, malignant HTN, drugs, cancer-associated

questions.explanation: Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) is characterized by: (1) Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) - schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, indirect hyperbilirubinemia; (2) Thrombocytopenia - consumption; (3) Organ dysfunction - renal, neurologic, cardiac. Major causes: TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%), Typical HUS (STEC-associated, diarrhea), Atypical HUS (complement-mediated), DIC (abnormal coagulation), HELLP, Malignant HTN, Drug-induced (quinine, gemcitabine), Cancer-associated.

whyChain.answer: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) form when red cells shear across fibrin/platelet strands deposited in small vessels. This mechanical fragmentation is the hallmark of MAHA. In TTP, ultra-large vWF multimers create platelet-rich microthrombi that cause this shearing.

whyChain.why: Why are coagulation studies normal here?
whyChain.answer: CRITICAL distinction: In TTP/HUS, PT/PTT/fibrinogen are NORMAL because the coagulation cascade is NOT activated - it's a primary platelet/endothelial problem. In DIC, PT/PTT are prolonged and fibrinogen is low because clotting factors are consumed. This helps differentiate TTP from DIC.

questions.stem: You need to rapidly assess whether this patient has TTP before ADAMTS13 results return.
questions.question: What is the PLASMIC score and how is it used?
questions.hint: Consider the components and what score suggests high probability.

options.text: PLASMIC predicts severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10%); Components: Platelets <30K, Hemolysis, No active cancer, No transplant, MCV <90, INR <1.5, Creatinine <2.0; Score 6-7 = high risk (~72% PPV), guides empiric treatment

questions.explanation: PLASMIC Score (1 point each): P - Platelet count <30,000 (1); L - hemoLysis (elevated retics, undetectable haptoglobin, or indirect bili >2) (1); A - No Active cancer (1); S - No Stem cell or organ transplant (1); M - MCV <90 fL (1); I - INR <1.5 (1); C - Creatinine <2.0 mg/dL (1). Score 0-4 = low risk, 5 = intermediate, 6-7 = HIGH risk for ADAMTS13 <10% (TTP). High PLASMIC supports starting plasma exchange before ADAMTS13 results.

whyChain.why: What is this patient's PLASMIC score?
whyChain.answer: Platelets 12K (<30K) = 1. Hemolysis (undetectable haptoglobin, indirect bili 3.8) = 1. No active cancer = 1. No transplant = 1. MCV (not given, assume normal <90) = 1. INR 1.0 (<1.5) = 1. Creatinine 1.1 (<2.0) = 1. TOTAL = 7 (HIGH RISK). This strongly suggests TTP - start treatment immediately.

whyChain.why: Why is creatinine part of the score?
whyChain.answer: Creatinine <2.0 favors TTP over HUS. In TTP, neurologic involvement predominates; in HUS, renal failure predominates. High creatinine makes HUS or other TMA more likely than TTP. This patient has normal creatinine with neuro symptoms - classic TTP pattern.

questions.stem: You are explaining to the team why this patient developed TTP.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of TTP and the role of ADAMTS13?
questions.hint: Consider what ADAMTS13 normally does and what happens when deficient.

options.text: ADAMTS13 normally cleaves ultra-large vWF multimers; Deficiency (<10%) → accumulation of ultra-large vWF → spontaneous platelet binding → platelet-rich microthrombi → organ ischemia; Acquired iTTP (autoantibodies, ~95%) vs Congenital TTP (mutation, ~5%)
TTP is caused by clotting factor deficiency
TTP is caused by complement activation

questions.explanation: TTP pathophysiology: ADAMTS13 (a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with thrombospondin type 1 motif) normally cleaves ultra-large von Willebrand factor (vWF) multimers released from endothelium. When ADAMTS13 <10%: (1) Ultra-large vWF accumulates; (2) vWF multimers spontaneously bind platelets; (3) Platelet-rich microthrombi form in small vessels; (4) Microthrombi cause organ ischemia (brain, heart) and RBC fragmentation (MAHA). Acquired immune TTP (iTTP): autoantibodies against ADAMTS13 (~95% of cases). Congenital TTP: ADAMTS13 gene mutations (~5%).

whyChain.answer: Brain microvasculature is particularly vulnerable to vWF-platelet microthrombi. Neurologic symptoms range from confusion, headache, and focal deficits to seizures and coma. Neurologic involvement is more prominent in TTP than HUS (which affects kidneys more).

Often idiopathic, but triggers include: pregnancy, infections, autoimmune diseases (SLE), HIV, certain drugs (ticlopidine, quinine). Autoantibodies (usually IgG) inhibit ADAMTS13 activity or accelerate its clearance.

questions.stem: PLASMIC score is 7. You need to initiate treatment immediately.
questions.question: What is the initial treatment for suspected TTP?
questions.hint: Consider the urgency and what removes the problem.

options.text: PLASMA EXCHANGE (plasmapheresis) is the cornerstone - removes autoantibodies and ultra-large vWF, replaces ADAMTS13; Start IMMEDIATELY if high clinical suspicion, do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 results; Add corticosteroids (prednisone 1mg/kg or methylprednisolone)
Wait for ADAMTS13 before any treatment

questions.explanation: TTP treatment (high suspicion/confirmed): (1) PLASMA EXCHANGE (TPE): Cornerstone - removes autoantibodies and ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing ADAMTS13. Start IMMEDIATELY with high clinical suspicion (PLASMIC 6-7). Daily 1-1.5 plasma volume exchanges until sustained platelet recovery; (2) CORTICOSTEROIDS: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or IV methylprednisolone - immunosuppression; (3) DO NOT DELAY treatment waiting for ADAMTS13 results - untreated TTP has 90% mortality.

whyChain.answer: RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATION: Platelet transfusion in TTP is classically described as 'adding fuel to the fire' - transfused platelets may worsen microthrombi formation. Give only for life-threatening bleeding or before invasive procedures. Do not transfuse to correct thrombocytopenia alone.

whyChain.why: What if plasma exchange is not immediately available?
whyChain.answer: If TPE is delayed: give FFP infusion as temporizing measure (provides ADAMTS13), start steroids immediately, arrange emergent transfer to facility with TPE capability. Fresh frozen plasma is not a substitute for plasma exchange but can bridge.

questions.stem: ADAMTS13 returns at 3% with inhibitor present, confirming immune TTP.
questions.question: What additional therapies should be added for confirmed immune TTP (iTTP)?
questions.hint: Consider targeting the autoimmune process and the thrombotic cascade.

options.text: Add RITUXIMAB (anti-CD20, depletes B cells making autoantibodies) and consider CAPLACIZUMAB (anti-vWF, blocks platelet-vWF interaction); Continue TPE and steroids; Monitor ADAMTS13 activity

questions.explanation: Confirmed iTTP treatment: (1) RITUXIMAB: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depletes B cells producing anti-ADAMTS13 autoantibodies. Standard 375 mg/m² weekly x4 doses. Reduces relapse rate and duration of TPE; (2) CAPLACIZUMAB: Anti-vWF nanobody that blocks vWF-platelet interaction. Faster platelet recovery, fewer organ complications, shorter TPE. Consider especially if neurologic involvement or refractory; (3) Continue TPE until platelet count >150K for 2+ days; (4) Monitor ADAMTS13 activity during remission - relapse occurs in ~30-50%. This is the modern standard of care for iTTP.

whyChain.answer: ISTH guidelines: If ADAMTS13 results expected within 72 hours and high pretest probability, can start caplacizumab. Greatest benefit when started within 3 days of TMA recognition. If ADAMTS13 >20%, stop caplacizumab. Risk: increased bleeding (11% vs 1% placebo).

questions.stem: A different patient - a 4-year-old with bloody diarrhea for 5 days now presenting with pallor and decreased urine output.
questions.question: What distinguishes typical (STEC) HUS from TTP?
questions.hint: Consider the clinical presentation and ADAMTS13 level.

options.text: STEC-HUS: Preceded by bloody diarrhea (E. coli O157:H7), affects children primarily, RENAL failure predominates, ADAMTS13 is NORMAL (>10%); Treatment is SUPPORTIVE (no plasma exchange), avoid antibiotics in acute STEC infection
STEC-HUS and TTP are treated identically

questions.explanation: Typical (STEC) HUS: (1) Cause: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (usually O157:H7) from contaminated food; (2) Prodrome: Bloody diarrhea 5-10 days before TMA; (3) Population: Children (peak 6mo-4yr), can affect adults; (4) Organ involvement: RENAL failure predominates (oliguria, AKI); (5) ADAMTS13: NORMAL (>10%); (6) Treatment: SUPPORTIVE - no plasma exchange (doesn't help), avoid antibiotics during acute STEC (may increase toxin release), manage AKI (may need dialysis). Most recover with supportive care.

whyChain.why: Why avoid antibiotics in STEC infection?
whyChain.answer: Antibiotics may increase Shiga toxin release from dying bacteria, potentially worsening HUS. Meta-analyses suggest antibiotics don't prevent HUS and may increase risk. Supportive care is mainstay. However, once HUS is established, this concern is less relevant.

STEC-HUS: ~90% recover completely, ~3-5% mortality, ~10-30% have long-term renal complications. Worse prognosis with severe AKI, CNS involvement, prolonged anuria. Much better prognosis than untreated TTP.

questions.stem: Another patient: 28-year-old with TMA but no diarrhea, normal ADAMTS13, and progressive renal failure.
questions.question: What is atypical HUS (aHUS) and how is it treated?
questions.hint: Consider the underlying mechanism and targeted therapy.

options.text: aHUS: Complement-mediated TMA from mutations in complement regulatory genes (CFH, MCP, CFI) or anti-CFH antibodies; ADAMTS13 is NORMAL; Treatment: ECULIZUMAB (or ravulizumab) - C5 complement inhibitor; requires meningococcal vaccination
aHUS is treated with plasma exchange alone
No effective treatment exists for aHUS

questions.explanation: Atypical HUS (aHUS): (1) Mechanism: Uncontrolled alternative complement pathway activation causing endothelial injury; (2) Genetics: Mutations in complement regulators (CFH ~25%, MCP, CFI, C3, CFB) or anti-CFH autoantibodies; (3) Presentation: TMA without diarrhea, RENAL failure predominates, ADAMTS13 >10%; (4) Triggers: Infections, pregnancy, drugs; (5) Treatment: ECULIZUMAB (anti-C5 monoclonal antibody) or RAVULIZUMAB - blocks terminal complement. Dramatic response in most patients. MUST vaccinate against meningococcus (N. meningitidis) before/with treatment; (6) May need prophylactic antibiotics during treatment.

whyChain.answer: C5 inhibition blocks the terminal complement pathway needed to kill encapsulated bacteria, especially Neisseria meningitidis. Eculizumab carries BLACK BOX WARNING for meningococcal infection. Vaccinate ≥2 weeks before treatment if possible, and many protocols include prophylactic penicillin during treatment.

Controversial. Some patients can discontinue after remission, but relapse risk exists (especially with CFH mutations). Individualized decisions based on genetics, disease severity, and renal function. Restrictive/time-limited protocols are being studied.

questions.stem: You are teaching trainees to differentiate causes of TMA.
questions.question: How do you distinguish TTP, HUS, and DIC using laboratory findings?
questions.hint: Focus on coagulation studies and ADAMTS13.

options.text: TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, PT/PTT/fibrinogen NORMAL, neuro>renal; HUS: ADAMTS13 >10%, PT/PTT/fibrinogen NORMAL, renal>neuro; DIC: ADAMTS13 normal, PT/PTT PROLONGED, fibrinogen LOW, D-dimer HIGH
ADAMTS13 is low in all TMAs

questions.explanation: Key laboratory distinctions: TTP: ADAMTS13 <10% (diagnostic), PT/PTT/fibrinogen NORMAL, neurologic > renal involvement; HUS (STEC or aHUS): ADAMTS13 >10%, PT/PTT/fibrinogen NORMAL, renal >> neurologic involvement, ± diarrhea prodrome; DIC: ADAMTS13 usually normal, PT/PTT PROLONGED, fibrinogen LOW, D-dimer markedly elevated, requires underlying cause (sepsis, malignancy). This patient: normal coag studies + neuro involvement + PLASMIC 7 → strongly favors TTP.

whyChain.why: What about other causes of MAHA + thrombocytopenia?
whyChain.answer: Also consider: HELLP (pregnancy, elevated liver enzymes), Malignant hypertension (severe HTN, papilledema), Drug-induced TMA (quinine, gemcitabine, calcineurin inhibitors), Cancer-associated TMA, Catastrophic APS. History, clinical context, and specific testing help distinguish.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with suspected TTP.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach for this patient?
questions.hint: Consider immediate actions and confirmation strategy.

options.text: Immediate: Send ADAMTS13, start plasma exchange NOW (don't wait for results), give corticosteroids; When ADAMTS13 <10% confirmed: Add rituximab, consider caplacizumab; Avoid platelet transfusion unless life-threatening bleeding; Monitor for relapse long-term
Wait for ADAMTS13 before any treatment

questions.explanation: Complete management: (1) IMMEDIATELY: Draw ADAMTS13 activity + inhibitor (before TPE if possible), start PLASMA EXCHANGE (daily 1-1.5 volume), start CORTICOSTEROIDS (methylprednisolone 1g IV or prednisone 1 mg/kg); (2) WHEN ADAMTS13 <10% confirmed: Add RITUXIMAB 375 mg/m² weekly x4, consider CAPLACIZUMAB if available; (3) AVOID: Platelet transfusion (unless life-threatening bleeding); (4) MONITOR: Daily platelet count, LDH, response to TPE; (5) TPE DURATION: Continue until platelet >150K x2 days; (6) LONG-TERM: Monitor ADAMTS13 in remission, ~30-50% relapse risk, education on symptoms.

whyChain.answer: With plasma exchange: mortality reduced from 90% to 10-20%. With modern therapy (TPE + steroids + rituximab ± caplacizumab): excellent response rates. Long-term: relapse risk 30-50%, survivors may have cognitive issues, need ADAMTS13 monitoring. This patient should have good prognosis with prompt treatment.
